question.title: Survey Question
question.prompt: Thinking about upcoming U.S. elections, which statement comes closest to your view?

questionResults.label: I'm confident the elections will take place and be conducted fairly
I believe the elections will take place but have concerns about whether they'll be fair
I have serious doubts about whether the elections will take place as scheduled

topline.label: I believe the elections will take place but have concerns about whether they'll be fair
I have serious doubts about whether the elections will take place as scheduled
I'm confident the elections will take place and be conducted fairly

key_takeaways: Most Americans expected elections to take place, but confidence in fairness varied.
A substantial share reported concerns about fair administration even when expecting elections to proceed.
Serious doubts were concentrated in specific partisan and geographic groups.
Within-party demographic comparisons reduced some large headline gaps.
State-level variation suggests localized confidence challenges.
content.methodology_note: We estimate a machine learning ensemble that predicts opinion as a function of party, geography, age, sex, education, income, religion, race, and contextual variables. We generate predicted probabilities for detailed demographic cells and post-stratify them to the county, congressional district, state house, and state senate levels using turnout-adjusted population weights before aggregating to the national level. Reported differences (e.g., rural vs. urban) reflect differences in predicted support for those populations as they are composed—including their partisan and demographic makeup—rather than simple unweighted survey averages. As a result, rural–urban differences are not strictly ceteris paribus; they partly reflect differences in partisan composition. For ceteris paribus-style comparisons, we report within-group breakdowns and crosstabs (e.g., rural vs. urban within party).
